content.text: What Must Appear on a Dispensing Label?

content.body: The dispensing label is the primary source of medication information for the patient after they leave the pharmacy. An incomplete or unclear label can lead to medication errors, missed doses, or dangerous misuse. Each Caribbean island has specific legal requirements for what must appear on a dispensing label.
A prescription label acts as a personal medication guide, outlining exactly who the medicine is for, what it is, and how to use it safely.
To make it clear at a glance, we divide the core information into specific, standardized sections.

items: Pharmacy Information: The top of the label displays the dispensing pharmacy’s name, address, and phone number for easy refill requests or questions.
Rx Number: A unique identifying number for that specific prescription. You will use this number when calling the pharmacy for refills.
Prescription Date: The date the prescription was written or filled, which dictates when the medication expires.
Patient Name: The full name of the person or dependent for whom the medication was prescribed.
Prescribing Doctor: The name of the healthcare provider who issued the prescription.
Drug Name & Strength: Usually the largest text on the label. It features the medication's active ingredient (generic name) and its strength (e.g., 10 mg).
Brand Name: If a generic substitute was used, this section will state "Substituted For" followed by the original brand name.
Description: A physical description of the medication (e.g., shape, color, or markings) to help you verify the pill inside the bottle.
Directions: This is the most critical section. It dictates how much to take, how often, how long, and how to administer it (e.g., "Take one tablet by mouth twice daily for 7 days").
Route of Administration: Clarifies how it should be taken, such as "by mouth," "apply topically," or "inhale".
Total Quantity: The total amount of pills or liquid volume supplied in the container.
Refills: Indicates how many times you can refill the prescription and the final date before which refills must be claimed.
Expiration (Discard) Date: The date the medication may lose its effectiveness or degrade. Always discard unused medication after this date.
Safety Warnings: Information highlighting potential side effects, such as "May cause drowsiness," or specific instructions, such as "Take with food".
Auxiliary Labels: Bright, color-coded stickers placed by the pharmacist to draw attention to critical precautions (e.g., "Avoid alcohol" or "Do not chew or crush".

content.body: Jamaica's National Health Fund (NHF) programme strongly encourages generic prescribing and labelling. In Barbados, the National Drug Formulary also favours generic names. In Trinidad, both generic and brand names may appear. When in doubt, include both the generic and brand name on the label to avoid patient confusion, especially if a patient has been switched from a brand to a generic equivalent.
content.title: Generic vs Brand Name Labelling
